title: The Effects of Statins in Patients with Advanced-Stage Cancers -A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
text:
The Effects of Statins in Patients with
Advanced-Stage Cancers -A Systematic
Review and Meta-Analysis
 
Qiang Zhou1, Zhihua Jiao2, Yuxi Liu3, Peter N. Devreotes2, Zhenyu Zhang4*
 
1Shenzhen center for prehospital, China, 2Johns Hopkins University, United States, 3China Medical
University, China, 4Peking University, China
 
Submitted to Journal:
 
Frontiers in Oncology
 
Specialty Section:
 
Cancer Epidemiology and Prevention
 
Article type:
 
Systematic Review Article
 
Manuscript ID:
 
1234713
 
Received on:
 
05 Jun 2023
 
Revised on:
 
31 Jul 2023
 
Journal website link:
 
www.frontiersin.org
In review
 
 
 
Conflict of interest statement
 
The authors declare that the research was conducted in the absence of any commercial or financial
relationships that could be construed as a potential conflict of interest
 
 
 
Author contribution statement
 
All authors were involved in the study design, data interpretation, and wrote the paper. ZHJ and YXL were responsible for
searching the scientific literature, figure generation, data collection, data analysis and data screening. QZ was responsible for
drafting the manuscript. ZZ and PD supervised this project.
 
 
 
Keywords
 
Advanced-stage cancer, Statins, overall survival, Meta- analysis, Cancer
 
 
 
Abstract
Word count:
237
 
Statin therapy has been shown to reduce mortality in a wide range of cancer types and overall stages. Still, there is uncertainty
about its efficacy in increasing survival among advanced cancer patients. We conducted a meta-analysis with data from all studies
that compared the hazard ratio of overall survival, cancer-specific survival, and progression-free survival in patients with
advanced-stage cancer who receive statin therapy. Studies were selected from the PubMed, Embase, and Web of Science databases
from their inception to December 31, 2022. Cancer types are limited to those rarely screened during the annual examination and
more likely to develop into advanced stages, such as lung, pancreatic and ovarian cancers. This resulted in 27 studies eligible for
meta-analysis. Statin therapy was associated with a 26% decreased risk of overall survival (HR, 0.74; 95% CI, 0.67, 0.81), 26%
decreased risk of cancerspecific survival (HR, 0.74; 95% CI, 0.61-0.88), and 24% decreased risk of progression-free survival (HR,
0.76; 95% CI, 0.65-0.87) for advanced-stage cancer patients. The associations were not attenuated or reinforced by study design,
study regions, cancer types, or other medical care. Concomitant use of other anticancer medications did not result in confounding
effects. Statin therapy produces significant benefits on overall survival and cancer-specific survival. Although the benefits might be
lower than the approved immunotherapy medications, its cost-effectiveness could lead to dramatic health consequences.
Concomitant use of statin drugs as cancer treatments is highly recommended in future clinical trials.
 
 
 
Contribution to the field
Statin therapy has been shown to reduce mortality in a wide range of cancer types and overall stages. Still, there is uncertainty
about its efficacy in increasing survival among advanced cancer patients. We undertook a meta-analysis with data from all studies
that compare the hazard ratio of statin therapy for advanced-stage cancer patients.
 
 
 
 
 
Funding information
 
This research was supported by the Sanming Project of Medicine in Shenzhen (No. SZSM201911005).
 
 
 
Data availability statement
Generated Statement: The original contributions presented in the study are included in the article/supplementary material,
further inquiries can be directed to the corresponding author/s.

In review

Abstract 
27 
Statin therapy has been shown to reduce mortality in a wide range of cancer types and 
28 
overall stages. Still, there is uncertainty about its efficacy in increasing survival 
29 
among advanced cancer patients. We conducted a meta-analysis with data from all 
30 
studies that compared the hazard ratio of overall survival, cancer-specific survival, 
31 
and progression-free survival in patients with advanced-stage cancer who receive 
32 
statin therapy. Studies were selected from the PubMed, Embase, and Web of Science 
33 
databases from their inception to December 31, 2022. Cancer types are limited to 
34 
those rarely screened during the annual examination and more likely to develop into 
35 
advanced stages, such as lung, pancreatic and ovarian cancers. This resulted in 27 
36 
studies eligible for meta-analysis. Statin therapy was associated with a 26% decreased 
37 
risk of overall survival (HR, 0.74; 95% CI, 0.67, 0.81), 26% decreased risk of cancer-
38 
specific survival (HR, 0.74; 95% CI, 0.61-0.88), and 24% decreased risk of 
39 
progression-free survival (HR, 0.76; 95% CI, 0.65-0.87) for advanced-stage cancer 
40 
patients. The associations were not attenuated or reinforced by study design, study 
41 
regions, cancer types, or other medical care. Concomitant use of other anticancer 
42 
medications did not result in confounding effects. Statin therapy produces significant 
43 
benefits on overall survival and cancer-specific survival. Although the benefits might 
44 
be lower than the approved immunotherapy medications, its cost-effectiveness could 
45 
lead to dramatic health consequences. Concomitant use of statin drugs as cancer 
46 
treatments is highly recommended in future clinical trials.  
47 
 
 
48 
In review
 
3 
 
Introduction 
49 
Statins, also known as 3-hydroxy-3-methyl-glutaryl-coenzyme A (HMG-CoA) 
50 
reductase inhibitors, are a class of cholesterol-lowering medications that reduce the 
51 
risk of cardiovascular diseases. Statin use in the US has dramatically increased since 
52 
lovastatin was approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in 19871. In 
53 
addition to their clinical benefits for cardiovascular events, statins have been widely 
54 
investigated for cancer outcomes2-6. In 1996, an increased incidence of breast cancer 
55 
in patients given pravastatin was seen in the CARE trial2. Afterward, consolidated 
56 
results from several experimental studies and large, high-quality randomized trials 
57 
demonstrated that statins had beneficial effects on cancer prevention5, 7, 8. 
58 
Statins inhibit HMG coenzyme reductase, which converts HMG coenzyme A to 
59 
mevalonate, and, reduces the availability of cholesterol and isoprenoids9. In vitro 
60 
studies have suggested that these metabolites play a vital role in cancer cell 
61 
proliferation10-12. On the one hand, cholesterol present in membrane microdomains is 
62 
reported to be a prominent mediator of the Akt signaling pathway in cancer cells, 
63 
which contributes to cell survival13, 14. On the other hand, isoprenoids, especially 
64 
geranylgeranyl diphosphate (GGPP), are required for the posttranslational 
65 
modification of proteins that localize to the membrane. Some of these proteins, such 
66 
as Rap, Rho and certain Ras proteins, are involved in networks essential for cancer 
67 
cell survival15-17. Moreover, the inhibitory effects of statins, and in particular GGPP 
68 
depletion, on tumor suppression were observed in human cell lines and mouse 
69 
models17, 18. The beneficial effects of statins on inhibiting proliferation or killing 
70 
cancer cells provide a molecular basis for the potential application of statins for 
71 
cancer prevention in patients. 
72 
Statins have been shown to reduce cancer-related mortality 7, 19. However, few 
73 
In review
 
4 
 
clinical trials and observational studies have evaluated the protective effects of statins 
74 
in patients with advanced-stage caner 20-25. It has been reported that statin regimens 
75 
were associated with prolonged on median survival (18 months) compared with the 
76 
non-statin users (9 months) in patients with advanced hepatocellular carcinoma in a 
77 
randomized controlled trial on overall survival25. However, others have reported that 
78 
statins use was not associated with overall survival in patients with advanced 
79 
hepatocellular carcinoma and ovarian cancer21, 24. Because there are conflicting 
80 
reports, meta-analyses that only include patients with advanced-stage cancer are 
81 
needed to clarify the association between statins and mortality from advanced-stage 
82 
cancer. Currently, one meta-analysis is available to assess the effects of statins on 
83 
advanced cancer mortality, however, it is restricted to prostate cancer with androgen 
84 
deprivation therapy and only includes retrospective studies26. Aiming to more fully 
85 
understand how statins influence mortality in patients with advanced-stage cancer, we 
86 
conducted a meta-analysis by collecting data from patients with advanced cancer 
87 
(higher than stage 3 or metastatic) according to the American Joint Committee on 
88 
Cancer staging manual (AJCC) staging system and then investigated the overall 
89 
effects of statin therapy. Cancer types are limited to those that are rarely screened 
90 
during annual examination and more likely to develop into advanced stages. 
91 
Material and Methods 
92 
Search strategy and selection criteria 
93 
We conducted a comprehensive search of the PubMed, Embase, and Web of Science 
94 
databases from inception to December 31, 2022 (Figure 1). A combination of MeSH 
95 
terms and text words was used to identify published papers on the assessment of statin 
96 
use and survival in advanced cancer. The search strategy is shown in eAppendix 1. In 
97 
addition, we hand-searched the bibliographies of selected papers to identify additional 
98 
In review
 
5 
 
relevant studies. No study design or language restriction was applied. Studies were 
99 
selected based on the inclusion and exclusion criteria presented in Textbox 1. 
100 
Title and abstract screening were performed using Covidence. The full text of the 
101 
selected studies was reviewed to determine the eligibility of inclusion. Data extraction 
102 
and risk of bias analysis were performed independently by two authors (YXL and 
103 
ZHJ), with any disagreements resolved by consensus. 
104 
Data extraction and quality assessment 
105 
Data from each study were extracted using a standardised form, which included 
106 
information on study characteristics (study design, source, time period, sample size), 
107 
cancer (cancer type and stage), and drugs (statin type and dose). We contacted the 
108 
authors of the original papers if some information was missing or unclear.  
109 
We assessed the risk of bias using the Newcastle-Ottawa scale for observational 
110 
studies27, based on the items of selection, comparability of groups, and 
111 
exposure/outcome assessment. We applied criteria developed by the US Preventive 
112 
Services Task Force Procedure (USPSTF12) to rate the quality of RCTs based on 
113 
randomization methods, double-blind designs, and follow-up reporting28. More 
114 
detailed information on the quality assessment is available in the supplementary 
115 
material. 
116 
Data synthesis and analysis 
117 
Data abstracted included the year of publication, country, number of patients, study 
118 
period, study design, cancer types, cancer stage, statin generic name, follow-up time, 
119 
and primary outcomes. We conducted separate analyses for statin usage and overall 
120 
survival, cancer-specific survival, and progression-free survival among patients with 
121 
advanced-stage cancer. For studies combining statins with another treatment to 
122 
In review
 
6 
 
improve overall survival, we recalculated the independent effect of statins on the 
123 
survival rate. Because of the expected heterogeneity in population characteristics and 
124 
study methodology, a Q statistic with a value of p < .1 or an I2 statistic > 50% was 
125 
considered to indicate significant heterogeneity between studies. If significant 
126 
heterogeneity was present, a random-effects model of analysis was used; otherwise a 
127 
fixed-effects model of analysis was used to combine hazard ratios to account for both 
128 
between- and within-study variability.  
129 
We assessed publication bias graphically using funnel plots and statistically using 
130 
Eggers’ test. We assessed heterogeneity using the I2 statistic. We used subgroup 
131 
analysis to determine sources of heterogeneity. Subgroup analysis included study 
132 
design (RCT vs. observational studies), statin types (hydrophilic vs. lipophilic), study 
133 
regions (the United States vs. European vs. Asian countries), cancer types (digestive 
134 
system cancer vs. respiratory system cancer vs. reproductive system cancer), or 
135 
treatment regimen (use of statins alone vs. statins combined with other medications). 
136 
Statistical tests were 2-sided, and we used a significance threshold of P <.05. 
137 
Statistical analyses were performed using the meta module of STATA MP, version 16 
138 
(Stata Corp LP, College Station, TX). 
139 
Results 
140 
Literature Search Results 
141 
We identified 3,877 relevant randomized controlled trials and prospective and 
142 
retrospective cohort studies by searching three databases and reviewing relevant 
143 
bibliographies. We excluded 1,346 duplicate articles and an additional 2,250 articles 
144 
that did not fulfill the selection criteria. After reviewing the full text of the remaining 
145 
281 articles, 254 were excluded for several reasons, as shown in Figure 1. We 
146 
In review
 
7 
 
included 27 randomized controlled trials and observational studies in the final 
147 
analyses.  
148 
Characteristics of Identified Trials 
149 
The included studies involved a total of 163,005 participants from more than 17 
150 
countries and consisted of 3 randomized controlled trials and 24 observational studies 
151 
reported from April 2001 through December 2022. Among the studies, the median 
152 
follow-up period was 41.2 months (ranging from 3.1 to 87.6 months), with a daily 
153 
dose of statin ranging from 10 mg to 40 mg (Table 1). All three randomized 
154 
controlled trials were designed with simvastatin29-35. Among 24 observational studies, 
155 
one observational study used pravastatin as the single agent23. All the other 
156 
observational studies described several statins in their research and one study 
157 
evaluated the effects of each generic statin separately on survival rates36.     
158 
The studies evaluated the effects of statin usage among patients with advanced-
159 
stage cancer. There are eight studies for patients with the advanced stage prostate 
160 
cancer6, 36-42, four for the advanced stage pancreatic cancer43-46, five for advanced 
161 
stage lung cancer32, 34, 47-49, one for advanced hepatocellular carcinoma50, three for the 
162 
advanced stage ovarian cancer51-53, two for advanced stage gastric cancer23, 33, one for 
163 
advanced stage esophageal adenocarcinoma54, one for advanced stage glioma55, one 
164 
for advanced renal cell carcinoma56, and one for cancer patients with brain 
165 
metastasis56, 57. 
166 
Quality evaluation 
167 
Among the studies, the mean quality score evaluated by the New Castle-Ottawa Scale 
168 
was 8.0 for the observational studies (Table S1), with 9 points for nine studies, 8 
169 
points for six studies, and 6-7 points for nine studies that had lower scores for 
170 
In review
 
8 
 
outcome assessment. Among the clinical trials, there were two studies rated as “good” 
171 
and two studies rated as “fair” on the scale by the US Preventive Services Task Force 
172 
Procedure (Table S2). The “good” RCT studies generally used appropriate 
173 
randomization methods in the study design, while the “fair” ones consisted of some 
174 
limitations in study design, quality or precision.  
175 
Primary Analysis 
176 
Three RCT studies and twenty-one observational studies provided information on the 
177 
association between statin usage and the overall survival rate among advanced-stage 
178 
cancer patients. The pooled hazard ratio showed a significantly increased chance of 
179 
overall survival (HR, 0.74; 95% CI, 0.67-0.81), with evidence of substantial between-
180 
study heterogeneity (I2 85.4%, P < .001; Figure 2). Publication bias was not observed 
181 
(the funnel plot was symmetric, and Egger’s test P = .20) (Figure S1). No individual 
182 
study affected the overall estimate by more than 10% (Table S3).  
183 
Seven studies provided information on the association between statin usage and 
184 
the cancer-specific survival rate among advanced-stage cancer patients. The pooled 
185 
hazard ratio showed a significantly increased chance of cancer-specific survival (HR, 
186 
0.74; 95% CI, 0.60-0.89), with evidence of between-study heterogeneity (I2 91.0%, P 
187 
< .001; Figure 3). Visual inspection of the funnel plots revealed some asymmetry, but 
188 
Egger’s tests for asymmetry were not statistically significant (P = .92) (Figure S2). 
189 
As the power of Egger’s test will be low with small numbers of studies, we used trim-
190 
and-fill analysis to impute the omitted studies. The imputed estimation was consistent 
191 
with the main result, with a pooled hazard ratio of 0.85 (95% CI, 0.48-1.22) (Figure 
192 
S3). No individual study affected the overall estimate by more than 10% (Table S4).     
193 
In review
 
9 
 
Seven studies provided information on the association between statin usage and 
194 
the progression-free survival rate among advanced-stage cancer patients. The pooled 
195 
hazard ratio showed that the increased chance of progression-free survival was 0.76 
196 
(95% CI, 0.65, 0.87), with evidence of between-study heterogeneity (I2 55.8 %, P 
197 
= .04; Figure 4). Publication bias was not observed (Funnel plot is symmetric and 
198 
Egger’s test P = .59) (Figure S4). No individual study affected the overall estimate by 
199 
more than 10% (Table S5).  
200 
Subgroup Analysis 
201 
We performed a number of subgroup analyses according to study design, statin types, 
202 
study regions and study quality (Table 2). In the analysis of overall survival, there 
203 
were statistically significant differences between different study designs, and the HR 
204 
of the observational study was lower (0.73 vs 0.95). Because the effects of statins on 
205 
the survival rate might change with cancer type, we also conducted a subgroup 
206 
analysis among the digestive system, respiratory system, reproductive system and 
207 
others. No statistically significant differences were demonstrated. In addition, in some 
208 
of the selected studies, patients were allocated statins and other medications, including 
209 
the simultaneous use of aspirin. Even though we calculated the independent effect of 
210 
statins, there might be the possibility of confounding effects. As a result, we also 
211 
performed subgroup analysis of the treatment regimen (use of statins alone vs statins 
212 
combined with other medications). No statistically significant differences were 
213 
demonstrated in any of the three survival analyses.  
214 
Discussion 
215 
In this systematic review and meta-analysis, statin treatment was associated with a 
216 
decreased risk of overall mortality, cancer-specific survival and progression-free 
217 
In review
 
10 
 
mortality for advanced-stage cancer patients. The associations were not attenuated or 
218 
reinforced by study regions, cancer types and other medical care, except for the statin 
219 
types. Concomitant use of other anticancer medications did not result in confounding 
220 
effects. The results of this meta-analysis help to clarify the effects of statins on cancer 
221 
survival in advanced-stage cancer patients and promote more eligible randomized 
222 
trials with large sample sizes to be performed in the future.    
223 
The underlying mechanisms responsible for the reduced mortality by statins for 
224 
advanced-stage cancer were attributable mainly to growth suppression, apoptosis 
225 
induction, and antimetastatic effects. First, previous in vitro studies have 
226 
demonstrated that statins can halt cancer cell proliferation by inducing G0/G1 or 
227 
G2/M arrest. The involved pathways include reduction of CDK4/6 and cyclin D158, 
228 
blocking the CDK2/cyclin E-mediated G1/S transition59, preventing the DNA-binding 
229 
activity of NF-ĸB60, and inhibiting DNA methyltransferases61. Second, the induction 
230 
of apoptosis was ascribed to decreased protein levels of anti-apoptotic proteins such 
231 
as Bcl-2 and Bcl-xL62, 63 and the activation of pro-apoptotic molecules such as Bax, 
232 
Bad, and Caspases 3, 8, and 962-65. In particular, our latest studies further explored the 
233 
underlying mechanism of inducing apoptosis by statins, it was found that the 
234 
depletion of GGPP rather than FPP blocked macropinocytosis, which serves as an 
235 
important route for tumor nutrient uptake. Defects in macropinocytosis by statins 
236 
result in protein and amino acid starvation, which further induces apoptosis17. Finally, 
237 
because metastases at distant sites rather than the primary tumors cause the majority 
238 
of patients’ death66, inhibition of metastasis by statins accounts important for the 
239 
inverse association that we observed between statin use and the mortality of 
240 
advanced-stage cancer. It was reported that the depletion of GGPP by statins blocks 
241 
posttranslational modification of multiple small GTPase proteins to localize to the 
242 
In review
 
11 
 
membrane. These small GTPase proteins such as RhoA, Ras and Rac are involved in 
243 
cell migration and tumor invasiveness17, 18.   
244 
We included 11 studies using other medications combined with statins for 
245 
therapy, so there is the possibility of confounding effects from other treatments. 
246 
However, we were able to control the suspected factors that may co-occur with the 
247 
use of statins by performing subgroup analysis. Comparing the groups using statins 
248 
alone with the groups using combination therapy, although the hazard ratios for using 
249 
statins alone were slightly higher than those of the combination groups in the two 
250 
types of survival analyses, there was no significant difference, indicating a lack of 
251 
confounding effect by using other medications together with statins.  
252 
In the subgroup analysis, we were able to evaluate several potential factors that 
253 
may affect the inverse association between statin therapy and mortality in advanced-
254 
stage cancer. RCT studies are commonly supposed to provide more robust evidence 
255 
for meta-analysis, but in the subgroup analysis of study design, observational studies 
256 
showed lower HR. Possible reasons include that these trials were designed to estimate 
257 
the improvement in the effectiveness of other first-line drugs, not the effectiveness of 
258 
statins alone, and more RCTs are needed to validate the robustness of our findings. In 
259 
addition, accumulating data suggested that lipophilic statins provide a stronger 
260 
protective effect than hydrophilic statins67-69. However, the hydrophilic group 
261 
displayed a lower hazard ratio in this study, probably because that most of the 
262 
observational studies did not clarify statin types in their studies, which limited the 
263 
number of included studies in this subgroup analysis. For the same reason, the 
264 
significant differences we observed between each generic statin were not powerful 
265 
enough to provide clinical implications, but these might be attributed to variations in 
266 
pharmacokinetic properties, dosage and treatment duration, genetic factors, 
267 
In review
 
12 
 
concomitant other medications, or patient population diversity. In terms of other 
268 
factors, such as study regions, study quality, and cancer types, no significant 
269 
differences were demonstrated except for the groups that included limited numbers of 
270 
studies. Therefore, the subgroup analyses indicated that most of these factors did not 
271 
attenuate or reinforce the association between statin treatment and the outcomes of 
272 
advanced-stage cancer. 
273 
Although the protective effects of statins associated with survival rates in 
274 
advanced cancer patients are lower than the approved immunotherapy medications 
275 
such as PD-1 or PD-L1 inhibitors (avelumab, atezolizumab, durvalumab, nivolumab, 
276 
and pembrolizumab)70-73, which display hazard ratios of 0.57 for PD-L1 positive 
277 
patients when compared with the conventional chemotherapy group70, Statins have 
278 
significant advantages as antitumour drugs. First, current lipid guidelines recommend 
279 
the use of statins to reduce LDL cholesterol, and people with a history of 
280 
cardiovascular disease or high LDL cholesterol are more likely to receive statins 
281 
without extensive clinical safety evaluation. Second, statins are much less expensive 
282 
than immunotherapy drugs. On a global scale, their cost-effectiveness could have a 
283 
dramatic impact on health. In addition, our study supports the conduct of clinical trials 
284 
to test the synergistic effect of statins with other approved anti-tumour drugs. In 
285 
addition, some studies have reported a high risk of cardiovascular disease in cancer 
286 
patients due to the cardiotoxicity of cancer therapy74, 75. However, more studies are 
287 
needed to evaluate whether statins can reduce these complications in cancer patients. 
288 
Identification of the optimal dose of statins to achieve more effectiveness in 
289 
reducing cancer mortality remains a key challenge. For most of the studies included in 
290 
this meta-analysis, statins were administered at a dose of 10-40 mg per day, which 
291 
was the moderate-intensity statin therapy dose recommended by the American 
292 
In review
 
13 
 
College of Cardiology/American Heart Association (ACC/AHA) for the management 
293 
of blood cholesterol76. High-intensity statin therapy has rarely been investigated for 
294 
advanced-stage cancer patients, probably because a higher dose of statins (80 mg 
295 
simvastatin per day) increases the risk of myopathy in myocardial infarction 
296 
patients77. In addition, a study from Denmark revealed that the cancer related 
297 
mortality for overall stages did not appear to decrease as the statin dose increased7. 
298 
However, basic studies indicated that a higher dose of statins killed cancer cells more 
299 
efficiently17, 18. Therefore, more trials are needed to clarify whether the effects of 
300 
reducing mortality by statins are dose dependent in advanced stage cancer patients.  
301 
Several limitations of our study need to be considered. First, there was significant 
302 
heterogeneity in the magnitude of association across studies, which could be due to 
303 
systemic differences in the study design, study location, characteristics of study 
304 
populations, statin types, stain half-life, metabolic site, and hydrophilicity and 
305 
cancer types. Nevertheless, in the sensitivity analysis excluding each study, our 
306 
overall pooled effect estimates remained similar, adding to the internal validity of the 
307 
conclusions. Second, there was a lack of evidence on longitudinal associations 
308 
between statin therapy and survival rate in advanced-stage cancer, probably because 
309 
of the considerably short survival time for advanced cancer patients. Third, a large 
310 
population of randomized clinical trials with available individual participant data are 
311 
required for reliable assessments of the association between different statins and 
312 
survival rates for advanced cancer patients.  
313 
In conclusion, statin therapy produces significant benefits in overall survival and 
314 
cancer-specific survival, irrespective of study design, study regions, cancer types and 
315 
other medical care. There is low-level evidence about the efficacy of statins on 
316 
progression-free survival in advanced-stage cancer. The concomitant use of statins 
317 
In review
 
14 
 
drugs as a cancer treatment may be considered in future clinical trials. 
318 
 
 
319 
In review
 
15
